nativeRange.habitat: The Emperor Angelfish inhabits coral reefs across a wide depth range of 1 to 100 meters, favoring reef fronts with dense coral growth, outer lagoon patch reefs, and areas with caves or ledges that provide shelter.

diet.notes: Requires a varied diet including Spirulina, marine algae, high-quality angelfish preparations, mysis or frozen shrimp, and other meaty items such as sponge-based foods. In the wild feeds primarily on sponges and other encrusting organisms, as well as tunicates.

avoidWith: other semi-aggressive angelfish of similar size

summary: The Emperor Angelfish is among the most visually striking marine fish, known for a dramatic juvenile-to-adult color transformation. Large, semi-aggressive, and not reef-safe, it is best suited to experienced keepers with very large saltwater systems.
careNotes:
The Emperor Angelfish is one of the most iconic species in the marine aquarium hobby, known above all for its dramatic color transformation from juvenile to adult. Juveniles display a black body covered in circular blue-and-white stripes, while adults develop bright yellow horizontal stripes across a blue body with an orange-yellow tail. This transformation typically begins once the fish reaches around 3 to 5 cm and may take 24 to 30 months to complete in captivity; adult coloration can be less vivid in a tank than in the wild.

This species requires a minimum aquarium of 833 liters (220 gallons) furnished with extensive live rock, deep caves for shelter, and ample open water for swimming. It is widely considered incompatible with reef aquariums — it tends to nip at stony and soft corals, clam mantles, and other sessile invertebrates, though it may be kept with small-polyped stony corals and certain noxious soft corals in some cases. Only one Emperor Angelfish should be kept per system, as the species is regarded as extremely aggressive toward conspecifics (members of its own species). In the wild, it is known to perform cleaning behavior, picking parasites from larger fish.
breedingNotes: The Emperor Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite in the wild, meaning females are capable of transitioning into males. Captive breeding has not been documented under standard home aquarium conditions and is considered essentially unachievable in that setting.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (40 cm SL vs LiveAquaria 15"/~38 cm — minor disagreement, conservative range 30–40 cm used), temperature (24.6–29°C vs LiveAquaria 22–26°C — broader conservative range applied), native range, taxonomy, and lifespan (max 14 years recorded).

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite in the wild (females can transition to males). Captive breeding is essentially undocumented and not achievable under standard home aquarium conditions.
